topic: weather patterns — when the rains will come

turns.content: my friend. the sky has been clear for too long. I am watching every day but no rain. can you tell me when the rains should come to Ashanti Region this year

The major rainy season in the Ashanti Region typically starts in late March to April and peaks around May-June. If you're already in May and haven't seen significant rainfall, that is concerning but not unheard of. Some years the onset is delayed by 2-4 weeks.

I don't have access to live weather data, but I'd suggest checking the Ghana Meteorological Agency (GMet) seasonal forecast if you can. They usually issue updates on the expected onset.

Are your crops showing stress from the dry conditions?
the young trees yes. the older ones they manage. their roots go deep. but the young ones I planted last year the leaves are curling. I water by hand some mornings but it is not enough

Curling leaves on young trees is a clear sign of water stress. Hand watering helps but you're right, it can't replace proper rainfall for a whole planting.

A few things to help them survive until the rains come:

1. **Mulch heavily** around the base of the young trees — cocoa pod husks, dried leaves, grass. This keeps moisture in the soil much longer.
2. Water in the **early morning or late evening**, not midday. Less evaporation.
3. Focus your water on the most stressed trees first. You can't save them all equally with hand watering, so prioritize.

How far is your water source from the young planting?
the stream is maybe 400 metres. I carry with buckets. my back knows every step of that 400 metres my friend. hah. I have mulched with plantain leaves but they dry fast in this heat

400 metres with buckets is hard work. Your back is doing the job of a pump!

Plantain leaves dry out quickly because they're thin. Try these instead:

- **Cocoa pod husks** if you have them stored — they're thicker and decompose slowly, plus they add nutrients back to the soil.
- **Dried grass or thatch** piled 10-15cm thick around each tree base.
- Even **wood chips or bark** from any recent clearing.

The thicker and denser the mulch, the longer it holds moisture. You want at least a 1-metre radius around each young tree.

Have you considered running a simple gravity line from the stream? Even a basic hose downhill could save your back.
the stream is lower than the farm so gravity will not help. it goes the wrong way. obi nnim what Nyame plans with this weather. last year the rain came early. this year nothing

Ah, if the stream is lower than the farm, gravity won't work — you'd need a pump, which is an expense and needs fuel. Not ideal.

You're right that the unpredictability is the hardest part. Last year early, this year late — that pattern of variability has been increasing across West Africa.

A few longer-term thoughts:

- **Shade trees** between your cocoa rows help retain soil moisture and protect young trees from direct sun. Plantain or gliricidia work well.
- **Digging small basins** around each young tree to catch whatever rain does fall, so it soaks in instead of running off.

For now, keep doing what you're doing. The older trees surviving tells you the soil holds water at depth. The young ones just need to get their roots down there. Every bucket you carry is buying them time.
I have gliricidia between some rows yes. those ones are doing better you are right. the shade helps. I should have planted more before putting the cocoa in

That's a good observation and it confirms what research shows — cocoa under gliricidia shade uses about 30% less water because the soil stays cooler and loses less moisture to evaporation.

Don't be too hard on yourself. You can still plant gliricidia cuttings now in the rows that don't have them. They establish quickly — just push a fresh cutting into the ground and it will root. Even without rain, they're tough enough to survive on minimal water because they're nitrogen-fixing trees with deep roots.

When the rains do come, those cuttings will take off fast and your young cocoa will have shade by the next dry spell.
yes I will cut some gliricidia branches tomorrow and plant. that is a good plan. even if the rain delays another 2 weeks those cuttings will wait in the soil. they are patient trees

Exactly. Gliricidia is very forgiving — the cuttings can sit in dry soil and root as soon as moisture arrives. Choose thick branches, about 2-3cm diameter and 1-1.5 metres long. Push them at least 30cm into the ground so they're stable.
how many cuttings per row you think. I do not want to shade too much. cocoa still needs some sunlight

Good thinking — too much shade is as bad as too little. For gliricidia with cocoa, space the cuttings about 5-6 metres apart within the row. That gives dappled shade, not full cover. And you can always prune the gliricidia back if it gets too thick. The prunings make excellent mulch and green manure, so nothing is wasted.

You're making smart moves. The mulching, the shade trees, the hand watering — these are the things that separate a farmer who loses young trees from one who keeps them alive through a dry spell.
medaase my friend. I will do this. when the rain falls on one roof alone the whole village is dry. but Nyame will send the rain. he always does. good evening
Good evening. That is a fine way to look at it. The rain will come — and when it does, your young trees will be ready because you put in the work now. Take care of that back too. Good night.
